data.title: Conestoga Capital SMid Cap Composite Q1 2026 Commentary
data.description:
SMid Cap Composite – 1Q26 Performance & Attribution

The Conestoga SMid Cap Composite returned -10.24% net-of-fees in the first quarter, lagging the Russell 2500 Growth Index's return of -3.52%. Declining markets have historically provided a favorable relative tailwind for our strategy given our emphasis on high-quality, durable growth businesses and strong downside capture. This quarter, however, proved challenging as the market backdrop highlighted an increasing divergence between fundamental business performance and stock market outcomes. While many of our portfolio companies delivered solid operating results, investor preferences shifted toward businesses with more immediate earnings visibility and away from longer-duration growth profiles.

The primary drivers of underperformance were sector/industry-specific headwinds and stock selection challenges, most notably within Technology, Industrials, and Health Care. Positive sector allocation effects partially offset some of the losses. Profitability and quality factors outperformed during the quarter, with profitable companies generally holding up better than unprofitable businesses. However, this did not translate into relative outperformance for the strategy, as the market continued to pressure longer-duration growth equities.

This dynamic was most pronounced in the Technology sector, particularly the software industry, where valuation compression and reduced investor appetite for future-oriented earnings streams drove significant declines. As a result, our overweight to software was a meaningful headwind, detracting approximately 232 basis points from relative performance. From a stock selection standpoint, Q2 Holdings, Inc. (QTWO), Tyler Technologies, Inc. (TYL), and CCC Intelligent Solutions, Inc. (CCC), all software names, were the largest detractors. During the quarter, we selectively reduced our overweight to software, trimming or eliminating positions where valuation support had weakened while maintaining exposure to our highest-conviction holdings.

Within the Industrials sector, the breadth of our holdings was strong with 11 of the 18 names adding value to relative returns, including many of the portfolio's top contributors overall. Watsco, Inc. (WSO), UL Solutions, Inc. (ULS) and Construction Partners, Inc. (ROAD) were the biggest winners in the space. These businesses benefited from more tangible, near-term demand drivers and demonstrated resilience despite the broader market decline. It was the names we did not own that proved most challenging with high-flying stocks Comfort Systems USA, Inc. (FIX) and Bloom Energy Corp. (BE) detracting a combined -124 bps from relative returns.

Stock selection within Health Care was a headwind with Repligen Corp. (RGEN) and Stevanato Group SpA (STVN) weighing most heavily on results. These businesses continued to execute fundamentally, but their stock performance was pressured by a combination of valuation compression and more cautious forward expectations.

On the positive side, Basic Materials was most additive to relative performance with our only two positions in the sector, RBC Bearings, Inc. (RBC) and Balchem Corp. (BCPC), boosting returns. RBC was driven by continued strength in aerospace and defense, where demand remains robust and increasingly visible through a growing backlog. BCPC benefited from steady growth across segments and continued alignment with "better-for-you" consumer trends. Our underweight to Financials, a weak-performing sector in the quarter, also provided a benefit to relative returns.

Despite the recent period of underperformance, we believe the underlying fundamentals of our holdings remain intact, and we continue to focus on owning high-quality businesses with durable growth characteristics that we believe are well-positioned to compound value over the long term.

SMid Cap Composite – 1Q26 Top 5 Leaders

1. RBC Bearings, Inc. (RBC)

RBC produces highly engineered bearings and components for aerospace, defense, and industrial markets. Performance was driven by continued strength in aerospace and defense, where demand remains robust and increasingly visible through a growing backlog. The mix shift toward higher-value programs supported both growth and profitability, with aerospace and defense revenue increasing over 40% in the quarter. Investors were drawn to the combination of long-cycle exposure and consistent execution in an otherwise mixed industrial backdrop.

2. Balchem Corp. (BCPC)

BCPC provides specialty ingredients and nutrition solutions across food, pharma, and animal health markets. The quarter highlighted the durability of the model, with steady growth across segments and continued alignment with “better-for-you” consumer trends. Earnings growth remained consistent, with net income increasing nearly 17%, reflecting both pricing and favorable mix. In a more uncertain market, investors gravitated toward BCPC’s ability to compound earnings with relatively low volatility.

3. Watsco, Inc. (WSO)

WSO is a leading distributor of HVAC equipment and parts in North America. Shares outperformed despite a challenging demand environment, as investors focused on margin resilience, strong cash flow, and a more normal demand environment in 2026. The HVAC industry went through a significant refrigerant transition in 2025 which resulted in a destocking phenomenon in 2026. This was exacerbated by a consumer preference to repair instead of replace with the more expensive new equipment.

4. UL Solutions, Inc. (ULS)

ULS provides testing, inspection, certification, and software solutions across a wide range of industries. The stock moved higher as results demonstrated strong underlying growth and meaningful margin expansion. Revenue increased in the quarter, while adjusted EBITDA margins expanded 460 basis points, reflecting operating leverage and productivity improvements. The combination of steady organic growth, margin expansion, and exposure to long-term themes like electrification and AI-driven demand supported a favorable investor reaction.

5. Construction Partners, Inc. (ROAD)

ROAD is a vertically integrated infrastructure construction company focused on roadway projects across the Sunbelt. Shares outperformed following a strong start to fiscal 2026, driven by both robust organic growth and acquisition contributions. Revenue increased 44% year-over-year while adjusted EBITDA grew 63%, reflecting strong execution and operating leverage. Importantly, a record backlog and raised full-year guidance reinforced confidence in sustained demand and visibility, particularly against a favorable infrastructure spending backdrop.

SMid Cap Composite – 1Q26 Bottom 5 Laggards

1. Repligen Corp. (RGEN)

RGEN develops bioprocessing technologies used in the production of biologic drugs. Despite a solid quarter, the stock underperformed as investors focused on a more measured outlook and lingering concerns around end-market demand. While the company delivered 14% organic growth, guidance for 2026 called for a more moderate growth range and incorporated headwinds in gene therapy. The combination of strong recent performance but tempered forward expectations led to a more cautious investor response.

2. Casella Waste Systems, Inc. (CWST)

CWST provides solid waste collection, recycling, and resource management services. The stock underperformed as investors are growing impatient with the company’s margin trajectory. While Casella closed the year on a high note with 60 basis points of improvement in EBITDA margin, including 100 bps organic, the company guided to just 0-40 basis points in FY26. This is below the 25-50 basis points previewed on the third quarter earnings call and attributable largely to the pending closure of one of its landfills in New Hampshire. We expect to see upside to this guidance through gains at its most challenged Mid Atlantic region.

3. Q2 Holdings, Inc. (QTWO)

QTWO provides digital banking and fintech solutions to financial institutions. Despite solid execution, the stock lagged as investors focused on a deceleration in growth and a more moderate outlook. While profitability and margins improved meaningfully, revenue guidance of roughly 10% growth for 2026 suggested a more normalized growth trajectory. In a market that continues to favor re-acceleration stories, the combination of steady execution but tempered growth expectations weighed on sentiment.

4. Tyler Technologies, Inc. (TYL)

TYL provides software solutions to state and local governments. Shares underperformed not because of a clear fundamental break, but due to a lack of incremental positive surprise in a higher-expectations environment. The earnings release itself contained limited new financial detail, reinforcing a perception of steady but unspectacular execution. In a market favoring clearer acceleration or upside revisions, the absence of a compelling catalyst led to relative weakness despite the company’s consistent, high-quality profile.

5. Stevanato Group SpA (STVN)

STVN provides drug containment and delivery solutions to pharmaceutical and biotechnology companies. The stock struggled as investors weighed solid results against a mixed underlying business mix and moderating growth profile. While margins improved and high-value solutions continued to scale, overall revenue growth remained relatively modest at 5% in the quarter. Continued weakness in the Engineering segment and a transition toward higher-value products contributed to a more tempered market reaction. The stock is also pressured by the decline of oral vs. GLP-1s.

SMid Cap Composite – 1Q26 Buys*

1. UL Solutions, Inc. (ULS)

ULS provides testing, inspection, certification, and software solutions across a wide range of industries. Given what we believe is competitive positioning and historically steady growth characteristics, supported by increasing demand for safety, compliance, and regulatory standards, we purchased the stock. The company's asset-light model and re-occurring revenue characteristics provide attractive visibility, while ongoing margin expansion reflects improving scale and operational efficiency.

2. CACI International, Inc. (CACI)

CACI provides information solutions and services to the U.S. government, primarily in defense and intelligence. The company continues to benefit from strong demand for mission-critical technology and services, supported by a robust backlog and favorable budget environment. Its focus on higher-value programs and disciplined execution supports a durable growth profile and expanding margins.

SMid Cap Composite – 1Q26 Sells*

1. Paylocity Holding Corp. (PCTY)

PCTY provides cloud-based human capital management and payroll software. We sold the position as growth has moderated and competitive dynamics within the HCM space have intensified. While the company continues to generate solid results, we believe the risk-reward has become less attractive given a more mature growth profile and increasing competition.

2. SPS Commerce, Inc. (SPSC)

SPSC provides cloud-based supply chain management software that standardizes data exchange between retailers and suppliers. Despite a beat on Q4 2025 earnings, management issued a softer 2026 outlook, projecting revenue growth to decelerate to 7%—a notable step down from its historical mid-teens trajectory. This slowdown, combined with sequential declines in their 3P customer segment and lengthening sales cycles, affirmed concerns we have about the company's remaining total addressable market and its ability to sustain growth.

Conestoga added to positions on seven occasions and trimmed stocks on eight occasions during the first quarter.

*Portfolio holdings shown above experienced material activity during the quarter. Securities shown are provided for informational purposes only and should not be deemed a recommendation to buy or sell any security. Holdings and transaction activity are subject to change. There is no guarantee that any security discussed will be profitable.

Sectors are defined according to the ICB industry definitions. Composite sector weights reflect the aggregate weights of accounts in the Composite as of 3/31/26. Sector weights are shown before fees (weights are not performance). (Sources: FactSet, Conestoga.)

data.title: Kimberly-Clark: No Growth Plus No Growth Does Not Equal Growth
data.description:
At first glance, Kimberly-Clark (KMB) looks like a stock that I'd love to own as a risk-averse investor. The company behind Kleenex has a long history dating back to 1872. It sells essential everyday products that consumers still need to buy during recessions.

The stock's valuation is well below the market average, and it comes with a 5.24% dividend yield, which is significantly higher than that of the average consumer staples peer.

Most importantly, Kimberly-Clark belongs to the exclusive club of dividend kings, having paid a dividend for 92 consecutive years and increased its distributions every year for over 50 years. This might be one of the main reasons why investors keep holding the stock through thick and thin.

While having a long history and being a dividend king doesn't guarantee that a company is going to thrive forever, it's one quality attribute out of many that should be factored into any analysis. That said, the research shouldn't stop there.

Just because a company has a long history of dividend increases and is trading at a comparatively high dividend yield doesn't necessarily mean it's a compelling long-term "Buy." Investors in Walgreens and Leggett & Platt might agree with me here.

As a long-term investor, I'm interested in long-term trends. While others are sweating over quarterly earnings projections, I tend to look at how the company has been doing over different economic cycles.

If a company has had erratic cash flows or close to zero growth over decades, there better be some positive changes ahead to convince me that the future won't look like its past.

In the case of Kimberly-Clark, there are major changes coming, but after my initial research, I believe the potential downside is larger than the upside. I rate Kimberly-Clark a "Sell" due to close to zero growth over a decade, a high payout ratio, and, most importantly, the Kenvue acquisition at elevated multiples. Let's go through it one by one.

Close To Zero Growth For Decades

Investing in Kimberly-Clark is not exciting. The stock comes with a low beta, sells products that don't make headlines, and has notoriously low growth rates. There's nothing wrong with that, as long as we're not overpaying.

"Boring" businesses can yield great returns as long as they are bought at very low multiples and high dividend yields. In the case of Kimberly-Clark, however, the business is a little too unexciting, and the valuation is a little too high. Revenues and cash flows haven't grown much in a long time. Therefore, the current P/E ratio of 13.40x does not look as attractive as one might think.

If a business remains stagnant and has few growth catalysts going forward, a fair P/E should be around 10. If Kimberly-Clark traded at these levels, the dividend yield would be 7%. Since we can only expect low-single-digit growth, we'd arrive at a total return of 9% to 10%, which is the market average.

A Fair P/E Of 10, Are You Serious?

Let's compare the 2024 numbers with those from 2014 and 2013. I am using the previous fiscal year because 2025 saw some massive structural downsizing, which makes it less comparable.

In 2024, Kimberly-Clark had $20 billion in revenue and $3.2 billion in operating profit for an adjusted EPS of $7.30.

In 2014, the company had $19.7 billion in sales and $2.5 billion in operating profit. The year before, it was $2.9 billion in operating profit and $19.5 billion in sales. So, over more than a decade, sales grew by a total of 2.5%, or a CAGR of 0.23%. Operating profit grew by a CAGR of 0.92% over the same timeframe. It's essentially flat.

EPS was $5.53 in 2013 and $7.30 in 2024 for a meager CAGR of 2.56%. This is mostly the result of lower shares outstanding. I'd argue that the coming decade will be more challenging than the last one, as more consumers are turning to private labels. A business segment that Kimberly-Clark just divested because of its notoriously low margins.

But What About The Dividends?

As a dividend king, most investors are likely in it for the dividend, which grew at a CAGR of 3.36% over the last decade. This is slightly higher than the EPS growth rate, but there is obviously a limit to growing dividends faster than earnings over the long term.

The payout ratio is already at ~70% of adjusted earnings. This is in the upper range even for a defensive consumer staple. I doubt that the company could keep growing its dividends faster than EPS for much longer.

So, if Kimberly-Clark were to remain a standalone company, a P/E ratio around 10 would be appropriate. At the current valuation, we can only expect around 7% to 8% total returns (5.24% dividend yield + 2%-3% growth), which is quite low given that individual stocks carry risks, and we could get 4% almost risk-free.

But those are all hypothetical calculations, because Kimberly-Clark likely won't stay a standalone company for much longer. The trajectory of the company is going to change quite dramatically. In my opinion, not in a good way.

The Kimberly-Clark And Kenvue Merger

Kimberly-Clark announced late last year that they plan to buy Kenvue in a $48.7 billion deal. That's a huge acquisition for a company with a $32 billion market cap. On the day of the announcement, shares of Kenvue surged 12%, while Kimberly-Clark fell 14%. The stock never recovered from its lows, and in my opinion, rightly so.

A $40 billion price tag (on an equity basis) is not cheap for a company that generated just $1.47 billion in net income last fiscal year. That's a P/E ratio of 27.21x.

To justify the price, Kenvue would need plenty of growth, but where will it come from? From 2023 to 2025, revenues declined slightly from $15.44 billion to $15.12 billion. Net income, cash flows, and EPS are down as well.

Before the spinoff, Kenvue was the consumer segment of Johnson & Johnson. In 2013, it generated $14.7 billion in sales. That's a CAGR of 0.24% from 2013 to 2025. Will it become a high-growth engine for Kimberly-Clark after the acquisition? I have my doubts.

Johnson & Johnson likely got rid of it because of its slow growth and potential legal challenges. Paying a 27x earnings multiple for this type of business seems overly optimistic. Kenvue holds $7.4 billion in net debt. Steep for a company with around $1.5 billion in annual FCF and close to zero growth over the last decade.

Kenvue faces lawsuits. So here, we have a slow-growing business with legal challenges and high debt, yet Kimberly-Clark believes it's worth a 27x P/E. For comparison, that's a higher valuation (especially if we factor in debt) than McDonald's and Coca-Cola, arguably some of the strongest slow-growing companies out there.

The deal is financed mostly through equity. If your stock is trading at 13x earnings, and you acquire another company at 27x earnings, the valuation of the new entity increases. Keep in mind, Kenvue will be acquired at a $40 billion market cap, and Kimberly-Clark is trading at a $32 billion market cap.

Synergies Often Don't Materialize

Let's stay conservative and dismiss potential synergies. In reality, many of those projected synergies never materialize. If you're combining a paper and diaper company with a consumer health company with a potential legal overhang, I'd be cautious about making overly optimistic projections. The businesses are quite different, and Kimberly-Clark likely lacks the expertise in medical regulations.

So, let's say the new company will generate around $4 billion in earnings. That's $1.5 billion from Kenvue and $2.5 billion from Kimberly-Clark. Kimberly-Clark shareholders will own approximately 54% of the company. That's $2.16 billion in net income ($4 billion x 54%), down from the $2.5 billion before.

The new entity will hold roughly $20.7 billion in net debt. $7.4 billion from Kenvue, $6.5 billion from Kimberly-Clark, and an additional $6.8 billion from the acquisition. In my opinion, that's too high for a company that I expect to generate $4 billion in earnings with little, if any, growth.

Larger Does Not Necessarily Equal Better

Combining two large enterprises that essentially sell different products and might have different cultures makes me skeptical. The track record for large mergers isn't the best.

Kraft and Heinz didn't work out well, and neither did Bayer and Monsanto. Some of you might be old enough to remember Daimler-Benz and Chrysler, Sears and Kmart, or AOL and Time Warner. In many cases, they were selling similar products; Kenvue and Kimberly-Clark are not.

Both companies failed to grow in the past, and I remain skeptical that the combined entity can return to growth. No growth plus no growth does not equal growth.

Kimberly-Clark's current valuation isn't low enough to offer a margin of safety, especially if we consider the valuation after the merger. I rate it a "Sell." The dividend king's track record does not change my view, as the merger could be used as an excuse to cut the dividend to focus on debt repayment.

Risk Factors

In the stock market, we're betting on probabilities, not on certainties. I could be wrong with my analysis, and the synergies could be greater than I expect.

The new entity, due to its scale, might be able to increase margins and return to growth. In the best case, the merger could create a company similar to the giants in the industry, like Procter & Gamble.

Conclusion

I'm not against investing in boring but stable companies at the right valuation. In the case of Kimberly-Clark, however, the valuation and the risks are too high, especially if we factor in the expected growth rates and the Kenvue acquisition at steep multiples.

I remain skeptical that two slow-growth companies can turn to growth, given that they sell different products. Kenvue comes with some legal challenges, which further add to my concerns.

The math doesn't add up for me. We're looking at businesses that have been flat for a decade and might be facing a future of increased competition from private labels. There's no reason to settle for a company that is getting bigger, but likely not better.

data.title: Delivery Hero Still Does Not Have A Clear Path To Sustained Profitability
data.description:
Notes: Unless indicated otherwise, amounts in the article are in EUR. At the current exchange rate, one euro is approximately 1.18 U.S. dollars. All per-share figures refer to the DLVHF ticker and are in EUR.

Investment Thesis

I covered Delivery Hero (DLVHF) (DELHY) about a year ago on Seeking Alpha (Delivery Hero Needs To Show More Progress In Asia). I was cautious back then. While I saw that the company was making steady progress towards profitability, I noted that the company’s fragmented geographic footprint and its heavy reliance on the Middle East to subsidize losses elsewhere would make it difficult for the stock to make meaningful progress upwards.

Looking at the share price performance since then, this cautious stance turned out to be correct. Delivery Hero shares are down almost 20% (in US dollars; in the native Euro currency, the picture is worse) and have failed to keep pace with the broader technology market.

My current view remains that the recent optimism surrounding the company divestments and financing activities is misplaced. The market appears to be cheering for financial engineering rather than actual business development. By selling its Taiwan business to Grab (GRAB), one of its few mature and profitable markets in Asia, the company is effectively cannibalizing future growth potential to pay down current debt, in my view. Furthermore, the entry of Uber (UBER) as a minority shareholder via a secondary purchase from Prosus (PROSY) does not provide the company with fresh capital or operational synergies; instead, it underscores the ongoing pressure for market rationalization and the potential for eventual consolidation.

The core of my thesis remains that Delivery Hero is a collection of disparate businesses where the high-performing Middle Eastern segment carries the weight of a loss-making global corporate overhead. With the geopolitical situation in the Middle East reaching a state of extreme volatility, this dependency is now a major systemic risk. Until management can demonstrate that the core European and remaining Asian segments can achieve net profitability without constant asset disposals, the stock remains a speculative Hold rather than a recovery play.

Financial Results And 2026 Forecast

Delivery Hero reported its full-year 2025 results on March 26, 2026, presenting a set of numbers that, on the surface, showed continued operational progress. Total segment revenue for the year reached EUR 14.8 billion, representing a 23.1% increase on a constant currency basis. This growth was primarily attributed to the success of the Everyday App strategy, which integrates food delivery with a wider range of Quick Commerce services. The group adjusted EBITDA improved to EUR 903 million, an increase from the EUR 692.5 million reported in 2024.

However, a closer look at the 2025 annual report reveals that true net profitability remains elusive. The group reported a net loss of EUR 782.5 million for the fiscal year 2025, with EPS of approximately EUR -2.62. While this is an improvement from the EUR 882.4 million loss in 2024, it confirms that the company is still far from being a self-sustaining entity under GAAP standards.

FCF was reported at EUR 250 million, up from EUR 217 million in the prior year. It is critical for investors to recognize that this group-level FCF is entirely dependent on the Talabat subsidiary. In 2025, Talabat generated an adjusted FCF of USD 559 million. This means that the Middle Eastern business generated more than double the cash flow of the entire group, essentially funding the restructuring of the European operations and the ongoing capital expenditures. This reliance makes the company's liquidity position fragile; any significant operational disruption in the Middle East, particularly given the escalating geopolitical tensions and the risk of a broader conflict involving Iran, could significantly reduce the group free cash flow and, in a worst-case scenario, result in a liquidity issue.

For the fiscal year 2026, management has provided guidance that is likely seen by many analysts as conservative compared to the 2025 performance. The company expects GMV growth of 8-10%, down from the 9.0% like-for-like growth achieved in 2025. Total segment revenue growth is projected at 14-16%, a significant deceleration from the 23.1% growth recorded in 2025. Adjusted EBITDA is targeted between EUR 910 million and EUR 960 million. While above the EUR 903 million reached in 2025, the target missed the consensus analyst estimate of approximately EUR 993 million. Finally, free cash flow is expected to exceed EUR 200 million, which would also be a decrease from the EUR 250 million achieved in 2025.

The Talabat Dependency

The Talabat subsidiary, operating across the MENA region, remains the most vital asset in the Delivery Hero portfolio. In 2025, the MENA segment contributed USD 615 million to the group adjusted EBITDA. To put the significance of this subsidiary into perspective, Talabat reported a standalone net profit of USD 464 million in 2025, a year in which the parent group remained in a net loss position.

The reliance on Talabat is not just financial but also structural. The high population density and favorable labor market conditions in key markets like the UAE and Saudi Arabia allow Talabat to operate with unit economics that are simply not replicable in Europe or Asia. The FCF contribution from the MENA region is currently the only reason the group can claim to be free cash flow positive. Disruption to shipping routes, delivery logistics, or consumer confidence in the Gulf region could have a catastrophic impact on Delivery Hero's ability to service its debt.

While Delivery Hero does not break out exact revenue figures specifically for the UAE alone (reporting it within the MENA segment), the UAE is a primary driver of the Talabat revenue stream, along with Saudi Arabia. I have lived in Dubai for several years. Right or wrong, I have a personal view here, too. If I were an investor in Delivery Hero, I would have a close eye on the real estate market in Dubai. As of H1 2025, the Real Estate sector alone contributed 8.2% to Dubai’s GDP. When combined with the Construction sector (6.7%), the broader property industry accounted for approximately 15% of the total economy. According to a study from two IMF economists, the corresponding 2021 (pre-crisis) value in China was only 12-13%. I am very cautious about the immediate economic prospects in the UAE.

Recent Developments: Grab, Uber, And Debt

In March 2026, Delivery Hero announced an agreement to sell its Taiwan food delivery business to Grab for USD 600 million in cash (after a sale to Uber for USD 950 million in 2024 did not go through). The transaction reflects an implied EBITDA multiple of approximately 9.3x, based on a 2025 adjusted EBITDA of around EUR 60 million. To put this into perspective, if this same 9.3x multiple were applied to the entire company, using the group 2025 adjusted EBITDA of EUR 843 million (excluding the divested Taiwan operations), the implied enterprise value would stand at roughly EUR 7.84 billion.

If we add the new loan facility of USD 1.4 billion (see below) to the financial liabilities at the end of 2025, we get a gross debt of approximately EUR 5.13 billion. By deducting the pro forma cash of EUR 2.7 billion, we get to EUR 2.43 billion net debt.

After accounting for the group net debt, the resulting equity value of EUR 5.41 billion would imply a share price of approximately EUR 18.30. Given that the shares are currently trading above this level, it suggests the market is already pricing in a recovery premium that is not yet backed by organic performance in the remaining loss-making segments.

Parallel to this, Uber Technologies recently acquired a 4.5% stake in Delivery Hero. Uber purchased approximately 13.6 million shares from Prosus, Delivery Hero's largest shareholder, at a price of EUR 20.00 per share, totaling roughly EUR 270 million. While this confirms institutional interest, it is a secondary market transaction that does not provide Delivery Hero with fresh capital or change its operational trajectory. However, there is a potential for a further consolidation, although at what share price remains unclear.

Regarding the debt position, Delivery Hero had a total debt of approximately EUR 4.2 billion. In March, the company received a new senior secured Term Loan B facility totaling USD 1.4 billion. The facility matures in June 2032 and was intended to refinance upcoming convertible bond maturities due in April 2026 and January 2027. While this reduces immediate liquidity risk, the new loan carries an interest rate of Term SOFR plus 500 basis points.

SOFR stands for the Secured Overnight Financing Rate, which is the benchmark interest rate for dollar-denominated loans and derivatives that replaced LIBOR. It is based on the rates at which big financial institutions lend to each other overnight, using US Treasury bonds as collateral. As of April 2026, Term SOFR is approximately 3.64%. By adding the 500 basis points (or 5%) margin, Delivery Hero is paying an effective interest rate of roughly 8.64% on this USD 1.4 billion. This means that debt service could continue to eat into the group's free cash flow for the foreseeable future, as the interest burden alone could neutralize a majority of the projected EBITDA growth in 2026.

Upcoming Q1 2026 Results

Delivery Hero has scheduled its upcoming Q1 2026 trading update for April 30. Investors should look for two key indicators. First, the GMV growth in Asia. In late 2025, management talked about a return to growth in the region, but if Q1 2026 shows a return to stagnation or negative growth, it would prove that the Everyday App strategy is failing to offset the structural decline in mature Asian markets like South Korea.

Second, and more importantly, investors must monitor the margins in the MENA segment and remain focused on the Q1 2026 results for any signs of margin erosion in the Middle East. In February 2026, the Talabat subsidiary issued guidance for the year ahead that pointed toward a contraction in adjusted EBITDA margins to a range of 4.4% to 4.8%, down from the 6.5% seen in 2025. This contraction is attributed to intensified competition in the UAE and Qatar. If the Q1 results confirm that Talabat margins are indeed declining, the entire financial structure of Delivery Hero could come under pressure, as there is no other segment currently capable of making up for the shortfall.

Risks To The Hold Rating

My cautious stance could be disproved if several factors align in favor of Delivery Hero. First, a significant de-escalation of tensions in the Middle East would likely lead to a re-rating of the stock, as the primary risk premium associated with the Talabat cash flows would diminish. Second, the company could execute a full spin-off or further secondary sales of its remaining 85% stake in Talabat. While Delivery Hero already IPOed a 15% stake, a full spin-off would involve distributing the remaining shares directly to current shareholders or selling them on the open market. A separate market valuation for the MENA business would likely result in a sum-of-the-parts value that is significantly higher than the current market capitalization of the entire group.

Third, a full takeover remains a possibility. With Uber now holding a 4.5% stake and activist investors like Aspex Management pushing for more aggressive restructuring, a bid for the whole group cannot be ruled out. If a competitor decides that the current valuation is an attractive entry point to acquire the leading market positions in the Middle East and Korea, shareholders could receive a substantial takeover premium.

Finally, if the Integrated Verticals segment begins to contribute meaningful cash flow in 2026, the reliance on Talabat would decrease.

Conclusion

Delivery Hero has made progress in securing its near-term liquidity, but it has done so by selling off quality assets and taking on debt at a high interest rate. The achievement of positive free cash flow is a milestone that is currently entirely dependent on a single geographic region facing unprecedented geopolitical risks and increasing margin pressure.

The sale of the Taiwan business at a 9.3x EBITDA multiple reveals the value within the portfolio, yet the hypothetical group valuation based on this multiple remains far from being realized due to the structural losses and high debt in other regions. Until management can prove it can grow without sacrificing its best assets and achieve group-wide net profitability, the stock remains a Hold, in my view.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Frontline: Why High Rates Will Last - Initiating Strong Buy
data.description:
Frontline plc (FRO) is the largest listed pure play on crude oil tankers. It owns and operates a fleet of 81 crude oil tankers, with VLCC rates reaching a historic high of $400,000 per day when the blockade on the Strait of Hormuz was announced.

I assert that the upside has not been fully priced despite the countless news articles surrounding the war and oil shortages and that the stock can rally further as high rates are structural in nature and thereby sustainable.

Upcoming Revenues Secured

Firstly, to determine how much cash the company will likely generate, it is important to note that management has fixed most of their operating days in Q1-2026 at exorbitant rates, taking advantage of a market that was already tight before the war.

The image below demonstrates the coverage as reported in the latest investor presentation.

Considering that the situation has gotten more and more volatile, it is safe to say that uncovered days will likely perform better or at least in line with the currently contracted TCE.

The VLCC and Suezmax contracted rates have increased by slightly over 40%. Meanwhile, the LR2 segment has almost doubled, with the potential to have captured more upside if we take into account that 33% of exposure was not contracted at the time.

Based on that alone, we can expect revenue to increase by at least 40-50% when compared to Q4-25, while operating costs are not expected to change materially.

Operational Analysis

When looking at fleet age, you will find something interesting about this company. While not necessarily a "newbuild" fleet, most of the ships fall within the 5-10 year age range. This is significantly younger than the estimated average age of 14 years for the global fleet, according to this article.

An important aspect to note is the recent transaction, which resulted in 9 new scrubber-fitted ships being exchanged for 8 significantly older ships within their VLCC segment. This efficiency upgrade cost the company $392.5 million and has seen all but 5 ships in the VLCC fleet be scrubber-fitted.

Scrubbers are important as they allow ships to use HSFO (High Sulfur Fuel Oil), which can, for all intents and purposes, be seen as cheaper but dirtier fuel. Recently, scrubber economics have been quite favorable, as ships that are forced to use VLSFO (Very Low Sulfur Fuel Oil) have been paying a steep price, according to the chart below.

This effect is only multiplied by the fact that the company's most common ship is the VLCC-class, which burns more fuel than their Suezmax or Aframax counterparts, increasing scrubber fuel savings.

Structural Supply Bottleneck

As was previously mentioned, the average age of the global fleet sits at 14 years, with a significant amount of those (upwards of 1 in 5) currently being above the age of 20.

Once you reach that age, they are more likely to be outright rejected from legitimate trade and therefore forced into the "Shadow Fleet". This is essentially a clandestine network of ships meant to facilitate trade for sanctioned countries such as Russia, Iran, and Venezuela (pre-regime change). According to this report, approximately 229 of all VLCC-class tankers are estimated to form part of this "Shadow Fleet." This represents over 25% of all tankers within the class.

Oil majors will not touch ships suspected of engaging in clandestine trade. This effectively removes a significant portion of all ships from the competition for providers such as Frontline, which deals exclusively in legal operations.

You might ask, "Surely shipping companies will be able to order new ships and replace aging capacity?" But that is where you would be wrong. According to this article, container ship orders have been so demanding that slots are being taken up until 2028 for large shipyards, meaning that any orders for crude tankers would have to wait a minimum of 2 years for delivery.

The Effect Of Geopolitics

As you might have heard time and time again, the Iran war and the subsequent blockade of the Strait of Hormuz have likely been the biggest catalysts in the history of the tanker market, with rates on some routes spiking over $400,000 a day.

Despite the ceasefire announced by the administration, the Strait of Hormuz remains blocked, which prevents route TD3 from operating. The only real alternatives for Asian countries, which are the most reliant on oil imports, are to take some of the longest voyages, such as TD22, as illustrated in the map below.

An important aspect to note is that the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz would not crash rates, as the tanker market was tight prior to the war and was expected to stay that way, according to this article published before the war. Re-escalation of the war is the cherry on top, which could result in rates spiking even more.

Projected Shareholder Yield

With the above points being made, all that is left is to calculate the yield. As previously stated, I expect revenues to increase by 40-50% QoQ based on the guidance provided by management, which would yield revenues of $874.3 million if we take the lower end of that range.

To be conservative, I will then assume that the cost of revenues will increase by 15% due to price hikes in bunker fuel and insurance premiums. This leaves us with $291.4 million in cost of revenues. When subtracted from revenue, this results in a gross profit of $582.9 million.

We then subtract approximately $160 million (assuming a slight increase in interest expenses due to the fleet exchange transaction) for various fixed operating and interest expenses, arriving at a net profit of $422.9.

Dividing this by the share count (222.6 million) gives a conservative EPS estimate of $1.9, which will most likely be fully paid out as a dividend according to the company's long-standing dividend policy.

This is a quarterly dividend yield of 5.3% based on rates that were locked in before the blockade. With spot rates even higher due to the ongoing blockade, I speculate that the company will likely do better in Q2, but it is too soon to tell.

I believe the quarterly yield can be faithfully extrapolated even in a pessimistic scenario, as I believe that the reason for the exorbitant rate guidance for Q1-26 was structural in nature, as pointed out in the previous section.

Price Action Risk

The main risk is not structural or fundamental, as I believe that those aspects are rock solid for the foreseeable future.

In fact, it is a function of this stock being in an industry that has attracted a lot of newcomers who believe the story is solely centered around the Strait of Hormuz. This may result in a correction of the share price, should peace talks be successful, despite my previous assertion that high rates are structural.

Taking into consideration a potential event-driven correction to the downside (which may happen even if not materially affecting rates), position sizing is important if you cannot stomach the short-term volatility.

Conclusion And Price Target

To conclude, I believe that the tanker market will remain strong due to an aging fleet and sanctions-related tightness. I am initiating the stock as a Strong Buy, despite the fact that stronger entry points may present themselves should my scenario in the previous section manifest.

My 12-month price target is at $50, which is based on annualizing my estimate for Q1 earnings and applying a 15% yield.

data.title: Tesla: Recovering Car Sales, Plus Non-Car Catalysts To Drive The Stock (Upgrade)
data.description:
The stock market continues to push toward brand new all-time highs in the wake of abundant macro uncertainty, but all of these gains are concentrated in a handful of chip stocks. Meanwhile, many other large-cap names have corrected substantially this year, despite positive earnings and an array of upside catalysts ahead.

Tesla, Inc. (TSLA), in my view, is one of these names. The world's largest EV maker has fallen ~15% since the start of the year on fears that EV demand is cooling. That narrative is starting to turn, with Tesla's deliveries in Q1 coming in ahead of expectations and skyrocketing oil prices making car buyers think twice about choosing an electric vehicle.

I last wrote a Buy article on Tesla in February, when the stock was trading at $430 per share. Since then, Tesla has crumbled ~15%, but in my view, the stage is set for a rebound in 2026. A recovery in Model 3/Y sales, particularly in Europe, plus improving gross margins and healthy profits, assures us that the car business remains healthy.

Investors have sold off the stock on CEO Elon Musk's statement that 2026 will require over $25 billion in capex spending to unlock factory capacity, particularly for two major new product lines that will begin production later this year: Cybercab and Optimus. These will be major catalysts that will begin to reinforce the narrative that Tesla is beyond a mere automaker, and after the latest dip, I'm upgrading my rating on Tesla to Buy.

Q1 download: rebounding 3/Y sales, strong FSD attach rates, and improving gross margins

Though the excitement for Tesla lies ahead in its non-consumer products that are slated to begin production this year, we still view the health of Tesla's car business to be instrumental in driving its brand and generating profit. Take a look at the Q1 earnings summary below:

Tesla's total revenue grew 16% y/y to $22.39 billion, which handily beat Wall Street's expectations of $22.23 billion (+15% y/y) by a one-point margin.

The chart below showcases the key metrics for the quarter. As was already disclosed before earnings in early April, the company delivered 341.9k units of Model 3/Y, rising 6% y/y. This represents a meaningful recovery from seasonally-higher Q4, in which deliveries fell -14% y/y.

The rebound in Model 3/Y sales changes my previously more cautious thesis on Tesla in a major way. Previously, I had feared that the fallout from Elon Musk's involvement in politics would dampen Tesla car sales for an extended period of time. Now, almost one year after Musk's rapid exit from DOGE, it appears that any negative backlash was short-lived.

Tesla's management notes that orders are skyrocketing in Europe, which was one of the major markets to show anti-Elon Musk sentiment. Buoyed by surging fuel prices and strong orders, Tesla's CFO Vaibhav Taneja noted on the Q1 earnings call that the company is at a 2-year high in open order backlog:

On the autos business, we have seen a resurgence in demand in EMEA and certain countries like France and Germany showing over 150% quarter-over-quarter growth in deliveries. In APAC, we witnessed growth in South Korea and Japan, again in terms of deliveries. Even out here in the U.S., we have seen a slight growth in terms of quarter-over-quarter deliveries.

On the order backlog front, we ended the quarter with the highest Q1 order backlog in over 2 years. Whilst the recent increase in gas prices has had a positive impact on the order rate, this improvement started before the uptrend in gas prices."

Another positive to highlight: the company added 180k net-new FSD subscriptions in the quarter, rising sharply versus 60k net adds in Q4 and 90k in Q3. Total active FSD subscribers are up 51% y/y to 1.28 million.

Though still a small slice of Tesla revenue, at the sticker price of $100/month, this represents $128 million in monthly revenue, and $1.54 billion in annual revenue. It's also an upside driver for Tesla's gross margins, which grew 478bps y/y and 110bps sequentially to 21.1% in Q1. Driven by strong vehicle sales and rising margins, we note as well that Tesla's adjusted EBITDA jumped 30% y/y to $3.67 billion.

Tesla's future extends just beyond consumer vehicle sales

To me, the more salient update that we received in Q1 is a firmer status on the company's new growth objectives. The market largely panned Tesla's decision to spend $25 billion on capex this year, which is a huge jump from $8.5 billion in FY25. Again, per Taneja's remarks on the Q1 earnings call:

So based on that, our current expectation for 2025 -- 2026 is over $25 billion of CapEx. And just to remind you, we are paying for 6 factories which were going to go into operation. Some have already started, some would go into operation later part of this year. We're further increasing our investment in AI-related initiatives, including the AI infrastructure to support Robotaxi and the launch of Optimus."

But there are two critical things to acknowledge. The first is that Tesla's cash flow pays for a majority of this expected investment: trailing operating cash flow in the last 12 months stacked up to $16.5 billion. And with Model 3/Y sales on a rising trajectory on top of improving gross margin profiles in the vehicle business after Tesla cut off Model S/X production, operating cash flow should continue to rise.

The second is that this capex is unlocking tremendous new monetization potential for the company.

Tesla Cybercab, which is expected to expand beyond its current test markets, is going to enter "volume production" this year. In the future, we don't expect Tesla to monetize this business solely through fare charging alone; we also see Tesla acting as an OEM partner to rideshare networks and selling wholesale AVs to them.

We're also enthused about the start of Optimus test production, with these lines eventually scaling to a capacity of 1 million units per year. Needless to say, Tesla's automation and robotics are still in the early stages, and yet they are the most advanced among any other company.

Key takeaways

It's true that, based on traditional valuation metrics alone, Tesla remains eye-wateringly expensive at its present >$1 trillion market cap. As shown in the chart below, Tesla sits at a ~90x forward adjusted EBITDA multiple.

Near-term multiples, however, don't fully capture the new product lines (which are essentially brand-new businesses) that Tesla is beginning production on this year. With the correction in share prices this year, I'm a buyer of Tesla again: relieved that the car business is rebounding and delivering the cash flow needed to finance factory expansion and looking ahead to the monetization potential from Cybercab and Optimus.

data.title: GPIQ Vs. FEPI: Downgrading Rex Shares In Favor Of Goldman Sachs
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Investors who build portfolios using various ETFs face a perennial dilemma: whether to pursue the highest possible dividend yield or to choose a more stable asset. Q1 2026 demonstrated that selecting funds with a sufficiently impressive "alpha" will not necessarily result in ideal investment vehicles. That's the case both when the market falls and during its V-shaped recovery. This article will analyze two popular funds: Goldman Sachs Nasdaq-100 Premium Income ETF (GPIQ) and REX FANG & Innovation Equity Premium Income ETF (FEPI).

Interim results for this year demonstrate that Goldman Sachs’ institution-based approach offers advantages that make it more effective than FEPI’s aggressive strategy in managing options. The analysis of the total return profile, as well as during market downturns and upturns, shows the superiority of GPIQ over FEPI regardless of the market phase. Although I was previously more cautious in my forecasts regarding GPIQ, now I can confidently say that instead of buying FEPI, you should increase your position in the Goldman Sachs fund. I have identified 4 reasons why this decision will be the right one for investors focused on both income and capital growth.

Previous Points

Before, when I looked at the GPIQ, first I saw how it compared to the JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPQ), but then I suggested cutting back on it by 50% to shift the freed-up capital into the NEOS Nasdaq-100 Hedged Equity Income ETF (QQQH). I made my latest decision due to the need to develop a safer investment strategy for the Nasdaq-100 (NDX), considering the ongoing escalation in the Middle East. As a matter of fact, for now, these concerns have not really materialized, with the sector demonstrating a confident resumption of the bull market.

I previously analyzed FEPI in comparison with the Roundhill Innovation-100 0DTE Covered Call Strategy ETF (QDTE), recommending the Rex Advisers fund. With the S&P 500 (SPX) broad market index rising 3.64%, the total return for FEPI stood at 4.53%. These results allowed us to gauge the broader market. But when compared to other sector-focused funds, it underperformed both during the market decline and during its sharp V-shaped recovery.

Analysis of the Total Return Profiles of GPIQ and FEPI in 2026

In analyzing the total return profiles of FEPI and GPIQ, let's take the Invesco QQQ Trust, Series 1 ETF (QQQ), and the State Street SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY) as benchmarks for comparison. As of the beginning of 2026, total return for FEPI was 5.04% higher than that of GPIQ. This is 0.41% lower than QQQ, yet it is quite impressive compared to SPY (given the short-term timeframe).

A question arises as to what drove these FEPI results during the market decline from February to mid-March, on the one hand, versus the subsequent V-shaped recovery, in contrast. We will analyze these two short market cycles to compare the funds’ performance depending on the direction of the trend.

FEPI and GPIQ outperformed QQQ during the market downturn, showing a decline that was 0.94% and 2.21% smaller, respectively. Although FEPI demonstrated slightly greater resilience during the sector’s decline, on the other hand, results for GPIQ were 1.27% better. We now turn to comparing the funds’ performance during the V-shaped recovery.

Once again, results for FEPI lag behind those of GPIQ, but only by 0.56%. Nevertheless, in terms of recovery, both funds underperformed QQQ by approximately 3.0–3.5%. The analysis of the total return profile in 2026 shows that GPIQ outperforms FEPI, in particular during a bear market. When the market recovers, though, both lag behind the broader market in that sector.

It is also important to note that, from the perspective of overall return, GPIQ’s results are more balanced, since they include both organic NAV growth and dividend generation. Meanwhile, FEPI focuses on "net cash," whereby all positive returns are derived from dividends. The change in NAV, in turn, has been negative since the beginning of 2026, at -5.38%. As a result, the total difference in price action between the funds stands at 9.22% in favor of GPIQ.

The Fundamental Concept Behind GPIQ

GPIQ fund, whose strategy involves selling call options. One advantage of this approach is the generation of premium income, allowing the fund to pay investors a high dividend yield of 10.68%. Furthermore, these options are sold on an index, allowing them to fall under Section 1256 (which provides more favorable tax treatment at a 60/40 rate). A disadvantage is the loss of a portion of profits during periods of aggressive market growth. It is precisely this that caused GPIQ to underperform the broader market in that sector in April 2026.

As a mechanism for selling call options, they use a flexible approach, with the sale of options accounting for 25–75% of the total portfolio. In February–March 2026, when the market was down, for example, they sold most of the portfolio. During the recovery in April 2026, options sales were reduced to the minimum limit. The generation of profit from options sales depends on the volatility of the index, so higher price fluctuations are not a risk but a resource.

Analyzing the portfolio structure, note that GPIQ tracks the Nasdaq-100. There are 102 holdings in the portfolio, of which the top 10 account for 48.58%. Information Technology makes up 54.71% of the portfolio, with Communication Services at 15.34%.

The Fundamental Concept Behind FEPI

An FEPI is a fund focused on the growth of stocks in sectors using the direct ownership method. That way, investors can receive dividends (albeit small ones) and keep the potential for asset appreciation. To top it off, they use an option strategy where they sell call options with long expiration dates. In many cases, these options have a one-month maturity, allowing for some flexibility to capture part of the upside before the yield cap is triggered. The actual performance of the total return profile in 2026, though, has shown that this advantage isn’t as clear-cut in practice.

Just like with GPIQ, volatility in the market is a key factor for generating income. When assets are perceived as more volatile, options become more expensive, which means you can make a higher profit by selling them. FEPI managers employ an active approach to managing the options strategy, allowing them the freedom to choose strike prices and the flexibility to select both in-the-money and out-of-the-money options. One of the fund's tax advantages is that 100% of its dividends are paid out as a Return of Capital. What's more, investors enjoy a high yield of 25.21%.

Looking at the portfolio structure, note that FEPI consists of only 15 holdings. In rebalancing the portfolio, managers follow a rule whereby each stock is weighted equally at 6.6%.

Comparison Table: GPIQ and FEPI

Refer to the table below to assess the differences between the funds based on a number of key metrics.

Advantages of GPIQ Over FEPI

Apart from analyzing the overall return profile, there are a bunch of other benefits to GPIQ that are super important for reaching the goal of generating steady income without the risk of losing your money.

Firstly, it has a super low expense ratio of 0.29%, way less than FEPI’s. If you’re investing for the long haul, this 0.36% difference in fees can make a big difference when you factor in compound interest.

Secondly, while GPIQ's portfolio is diversified and consists of 102 holdings, FEPI has built a portfolio comprising only 15 companies. That makes it extremely concentrated, which means that if one company's stock price drops by as little as 15–20%, that could lower the fund's total capital value by 1%.

Thirdly, GPIQ uses a flexible strategy to capture market growth, since option sales can be reduced to 25% of the portfolio’s weight. This allows the fund to combine a focus on both income and capital growth. In contrast, FEPI’s aggressive options strategy is designed to maximize profits, causing the fund to underperform during market rallies.

Finally, GPIQ's methodology of writing index options allows it to smooth out market volatility and generate a more predictable cash flow. FEPI sells options on individual stocks. While this allows it to generate higher returns, on the other hand, it exposes the fund to the additional risk of sharp price swings.

Market Outlook for the Tech Sector and Its Specific Impact on Options Strategies

I'm feeling more optimistic about the tech sector starting in Q2 2026, compared to a couple of months ago, thanks to the market entering a phase of "mature volatility." Factor fundamentals supporting the continuation of the AI supercycle outweigh all risks and threats arising from the external environment (geopolitics). Currently, tech sector companies, in spite of continued strong CapEx growth, demonstrate their focus on monetizing technologies and increasing business margins. As global and U.S. economic events continue to drive high price volatility, their market capitalization will continue to grow.

As the market remains in this state, more traditional call-selling strategies become not merely a source of additional income, nor merely a necessary buffer. It is the difference in the methodologies of GPIQ and FEPI’s option strategies that defines their total return profiles for the remainder of 2026. Considering the potential for a repeat of the sharp decline in the Nasdaq-100 index and a subsequent V-shaped recovery, I believe a FEPI strategy, in which call options on individual stocks are sold, is becoming an "anchor." That means investors miss out on price appreciation, like we saw in Q1 2026, when the NAV change was negative.

GPIQ has an advantage due to the fact that options are sold on the index itself. However, cover ratios vary between 25% and 75%. Ultimately, as the bull trend continues amid the AI supercycle, the active management of GPIQ will allow for a reduction in the proportion of options sold, positively impacting the capture of price appreciation. At the same time, Section 1256 will continue to provide tax benefits for investors. All in all, GPIQ is a great fit for a volatile bull market because its options strategy allows investors to simultaneously earn double-digit returns and take part in the growth of market capitalization in these sectors.

GPIQ Risks

I believe that when choosing GPIQ as an investment, its three main risks should be taken into account. But before I get into them, it's worth noting that each of these risks also applies to investments in FEPI.

  • Risk of growth ceiling, limiting the fund's returns. Its dynamic options strategy captures a significant portion of the index’s growth but still does not allow it to outperform the net QQQ index. Over the long term, it reduces the potential for capital growth while at the same time providing a degree of protection in the event of a market downturn.
  • Even though the portfolio includes 102 holdings, around half of the portfolio is comprised of the top 10. It shows a high concentration in the portfolio, plus it's tied to the continuation of the AI supercycle. Accordingly, in the event of problems specifically affecting the development of the computing infrastructure sector, fund shares could enter a phase of sharp decline.
  • Fund performance remains sensitive to interest rates. Inflation expectations are rising due to current events in the Middle East. These factors are leading to a revision of interest rate forecasts toward maintaining current rates. Should the market see a recurrence of factors driving up yields on 10-year U.S. Treasury bonds, this will result in an outflow of assets from GPIQ. It will coincide with the general market trend, in which NAV is likely to decline.

Conclusion

Comparing GPIQ and FEPI, which I previously recommended as "Buy," my current preference is for the Goldman Sachs fund, while I downgrade the Rex Advisers product to "Hold." First and foremost, it is the total return profile that highlights GPIQ’s advantages. The choice of this fund is more rational and safer because the Goldman Sachs team is capable of formulating an option strategy that is less resistant to growth. Furthermore, portfolio composition includes a broader fundamental base, with management costs optimized to a minimum. I consider FEPI to be a riskier instrument, underperforming GPIQ and the broader market in 2026, in both downturns and uptrends. As such, FEPI is a tactical tool within a sideways-moving market, with the sole objective of maximizing payouts. It is subject to higher risks when used for capital preservation.

data.title: Pzena Global Value ADR Q1 2026 Commentary
data.description:
Global equities began the quarter on solid footing but subsequently reversed course amid concerns surrounding the software sector, driven in part by new AI developments from Anthropic (ANTHRO). These concerns, coupled with an escalating conflict in the Middle East, contributed to broader unease about the global economy and the private credit market. The United States was our portfolio's weakest-performing region, declining mid-single digits. We outperformed the MSCI World Index, driven by our investments in the materials sector, where stock selection and an overweight position benefited performance. Our detracting sectors were financials, health care, and industrials.

The largest contributions came from the materials and energy sectors, as oil prices broke above $100 per barrel. Dow, Inc. (DOW) was the largest individual contributor, as approximately 65% of its ethane crackers utilize low-cost natural gas feedstock, positioning the company to benefit from higher oil prices. Samsung Electronics (SSNLF) was the second-largest contributor, as consensus estimates for 2026 and 2027 continued to rise, driven by a more than 100% increase in spot memory prices. Nokia (NOK) was higher on investor optimism surrounding its AI-RAN prospects, while its network infrastructure unit continued to exhibit solid growth.

The portfolio's largest detractors were in the financials and health care sectors. Cognizant (CTSH) reported strong bookings and market share gains, but investor focus shifted to the sharing of AI-driven productivity gains with clients, which weighed on the stock. Capital One detracted as management guided to higher expenses related to the rollout of the Discover Financial Services network, while broader concerns around private credit risks and a potential economic slowdown added pressure. Humana (HUM) detracted as the company disclosed an unexpected 25% increase in Medicare Advantage plan membership, eliciting concerns of adverse selection, though growth in its 4-star plan membership should help offset some of the added medical costs.

We added to our positions in Accenture (ACN), Publicis (PUBGY), and Daikin (DKILY), all on weakness. Publicis, like Accenture, has been caught up in the AI-disruption selloff that characterized this quarter. Daikin, a global leader in air conditioning systems, is facing macroeconomic uncertainty across several geographies and tariff pressures, and we believe there is potential for margin improvement and a renewed focus on capital efficiency. We initiated a position in PT Bank Rakyat Indonesia (BKRKY), an Indonesian commercial bank trading at a depressed valuation due to weak macroeconomic conditions and elevated losses in its microfinance segment. We view it as a high-quality franchise well positioned to participate in Indonesia's long-term growth despite near-term challenges. To fund these investments, we exited our position in Medtronic (MDT) on valuation and trimmed our positions in Barclays (BCS), Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Co. (TSMC) (TSM), ArcelorMittal (MT), and Dow on appreciation.

Recent performance has been challenged by a combination of macroeconomic concerns, uncertainty around the impact of technological change across industries, and idiosyncratic developments weighing on the short-term fundamentals of a few large, high-conviction holdings. Despite these headwinds, we believe the current environment provides an exceptional long-term opportunity, given the portfolio's starting valuation and the breadth, magnitude, and quality of the opportunity set.
