data.title: Alphabet Q1: First Signs Of Depreciation Pressuring EPS Are Here
data.description:
I am downgrading my rating on Alphabet Inc. (GOOG) (GOOGL) heading into the Q1 2026 earnings release on April 29th, as I'm concerned that the AI CapEx cycle is moving into the income statement.

Yes, I'm talking about depreciation, as those GPUs and TPUs are coming online. That has a double-edged sword.

On one hand, it’s a tailwind for the cloud segment, and I want to believe it may be one for Search as well. In fact, the latter is one of the factors that I will closely monitor in the Q1 print.

On the other hand, the Street is projecting a mid-single-digit decline in EPS in Q1. In other words, as hinted by CEO Sundar Pichai in Q4, depreciation is ticking up, and that's not good news for earnings, particularly when the company has been slow to lay off employees in comparison to Meta (META) or Microsoft (MSFT).

As I discuss in this piece, I don't think there is an asymmetric upside in buying prior to the Q1 print. Therefore, I downgrade to a Hold.

Three Factors I'm Monitoring In Q1 2026

First, I want to see progress on the AI front. Specifically, AI Search.

As seen above, the growth rate of Google Search (and other) revenue accelerated since Q1 2025. It's impossible to know whether that was driven by AI (think: Gemini-powered ad matching) or simply by stronger consumer spending.

Based on the chart above, which shows the change in real PCE in the U.S. over the last few years, I don’t think this factor drove Alphabet's growth rate in the past 12 months.

From what I'm reading in the last earnings call, back in Q4 2025, search usage hit a record, AI Mode queries per user in the US doubled since launch, and AI Mode queries were 3x longer than traditional searches.

As you may know, Google is already testing ads within AI Mode responses. Unfortunately, management hasn't disclosed any revenue figures yet from this new ad surface. If they do in Q1 (unlikely, as AI Mode ads are still in testing mode), I would monitor that figure going forward.

Second, I would closely monitor the cloud segment, which showed a spike in growth last quarter.

Google Cloud exited Q4 at a revenue run rate above $70B, with backlog up 55% sequentially and more than double on a YOY basis.

Notably, Cloud AI demand was broad, with nearly 75% of Google Cloud customers using Google’s vertically optimized AI, according to CEO Sundar Pichai during the last earnings call.

In fact, AI customers used 1.8x as many products as non-AI customers, which shows how important this cohort is for the top line of this segment.

Right after Q4, KeyBanc raised its Alphabet target to $380 and estimated 2026 Google Cloud revenue at $91.8B. For reference, the consensus back then was $85.3B.

Speaking of the consensus, right after Q4, the Street's revenue forecast for the next 4 quarters has been upgraded for the third consecutive time.

Needless to say, I want to see a similar upgrade this week. That may only happen if results come in above consensus.

For reference, I included above the Street expectations for revenue and below the Street's models for EPS.

I discuss the third factor in the next section.

Risks

The third factor that I will closely monitor is CapEx. Here, I don't want to see more upgrades, as the $175B-$185B of FY 2026 CapEx is already ridiculously high. For reference, the company spent only $91.4 billion in capital expenditures in 2025. At the midpoint of the FY26 guidance, we're talking about a 97% YOY increase.

To be clear, I am not bothered by the pressure on free cash flows or share buybacks. My main concern is EPS, which will likely face pressure from depreciation as data centers come online.

For reference, depreciation increased last year, and based on the Q4 earnings call, management expects it to “meaningfully increase for the full year.”

In 2025, depreciation increased by nearly $6 billion, or 38%, from $15.3 billion in 2024, to $21.1 billion in 2025. Given the increase in our CapEx investments in recent years, we expect the growth rate in 2026 depreciation to accelerate in Q1, and meaningfully increase for the full year.

Given the growth trend in D&A seen above, I wouldn't be surprised to see Google join the likes of Meta or Microsoft in the layoff wave this year. So far, Alphabet's layoff announcements this year are minimal.

Valuation

I am not particularly bothered about the 29x (call it 30x) forward P/E multiple.

Compared to the rest of the hyperscalers, Alphabet is the second most expensive on the valuation front, trailing Amazon.

Given that Amazon’s top-line growth is weaker than Google's and AWS growth still trails Google Cloud, I don’t see why Google can’t rerate to the mid-30s (forward P/E).

Looking Ahead

You probably remember what happens with a hyperscaler when they show proof that AI is lifting the top-line growth. I'm talking about Meta's 10% jump post Q4 earnings, a topic I covered in this piece.

Unfortunately, Alphabet doesn't guide revenue for the next quarter or even for the entire fiscal year. Therefore, all we have are management's comments on the AI strategy in Search and Cloud and the results from the last quarter.

As a "fun fact," the company has reported revenue above consensus since Q1 2025. I don't expect to see a diversion from this trend in Q1 2026.

That said, I am a bit concerned about the chart below.

Call me crazy, but that looks pretty much like a double top to me. I think the biggest risk in Q1 is a CapEx upgrade. If this happens while the Q1 beat is soft, I think the stock may sell off post-earnings.

Will that break the bull case?

In my view, it largely depends on whether the Cloud segment comes in below the Street's expectations. For reference, for the Q1 2026 print, the current Cloud revenue consensus appears to be about $18.4B, implying roughly 50% y/y growth.

Overall, I prefer to sit on my hands before the Q1 earnings release, as I want to see progress before I build my conviction on this stock again.

data.title: Rollins: Despite Margin Declines, I Remain Confident In This Niche Market Leader
data.description:
Earlier this year, I stated Rollins, Inc. (ROL) was a “Hold” as even though I believed Rollins was (and continues to be) a high-quality business, its valuation was a little too stretched for my liking.

So far in 2026, Rollins is down more than 5% YTD while the S&P 500 is up over 4%

For long-term investors seeking an exceptional business that can survive in the age of artificial intelligence, I believe Rollins fits the bill. Let’s dig into the company’s most recent quarter as I discuss why I’m building an initial position here.

Company Updates

Rollins made three acquisitions in the quarter for a total of $18 million. However, the company’s largest acquisition year-to-date occurred after the first quarter ended as Rollins purchased Romex Pest Control, LLC (Romex) for $90 million in early April. Romex is a pest control company with numerous locations in Texas, Oklahoma, Louisiana, and Mississippi.

Here’s what Rollins’ CEO Jerry Gahlhoff had to say about this acquisition on the company’s last earnings call:

Earlier this month, we announced our acquisition of Romex Pest Control, a top 40 pest management company according to PCT top 100 rankings. Romex provides us with entry points into new markets while enabling them to further scale their operations and expand service offerings to their existing customer base. Most importantly, they have a strong people and customer-focused culture, and we were thrilled to welcome our new Romex teammates to the Rollins family.

To me, this seems like another outstanding acquisition that gives Rollins further access to the southern part of the United States.

Next, I’d like to touch on another factor that I mentioned in my original piece about Rollins, and that’s the company’s ongoing investments in personnel and training. I think it’s an aspect of many businesses that often isn’t emphasized enough, but I believe it can play a major role in the quality of service a customer receives. I thought this was an interesting quote from Gahlhoff as he talked about how the business has become more complex over the years:

…When I started in this business decades ago, this business was a lot simpler. And I did pest control and I did termite work. And the options that we had -- I had to learn about and what I had to do were relatively simple 30 years ago. Today, you've heard Ken talk about having 9 shots on goal. And when you have to train people to be able to be experts and knowledgeable, both on the service and the sales side for the complexity of all the things that our team does, that takes time. It takes experience. It's harder and harder. You can't just get somebody up and running in a few weeks like it was 30 years ago when I started in this business.

High-quality training, I believe, translates into more knowledgeable and more confident employees, which will result in great service, which will increase the odds a customer would come back to Rollins again.

I also think there's an aspect of knowledge transfer that because Rollins has so many companies under their umbrella, they can share experiences and knowledge, making employees in the field better able to deal with numerous scenarios. This further enhances the company’s moat and reaffirms Rollins’ status as a top company in this niche market.

Financials

In Q1 2026, Rollins generated revenue of roughly $906 million, which is an increase of more than 10% compared to Q1 2025. Of this 10%, 6.6% was from organic growth, and the rest came from acquisitions.

As you can see below, compared to Q1 2025, operating income and net income grew just like total sales:

For the quarter, gross margins did decline, coming in at 50.8% compared to 51.4% in the prior year’s first quarter. Management noted this was due to higher insurance and claims as well as some payroll costs as the business is preparing for its peak season.

Management noted they expect margins to improve as business ramps up over the next few quarters. The management team did state they continue to believe their targets of 7% to 8% of organic revenue growth and 2% to 3% revenue growth from M&A will be achievable. Additionally, the team expects cash flow will continue to convert at a rate exceeding 100% for the rest of the current fiscal year.

Rollins shareholders can appreciate that weighted average shares outstanding have declined over the last twelve months and the company’s amount of dividends paid per share has increased. In the current quarter, Rollins paid dividends to shareholders totaling $88 million.

Rollins has a decent Balance Sheet as you can see below:

Rollins doesn’t have enough current assets to cover all of the company’s current liabilities, although a large portion of the company's current liabilities balance is unearned revenue.

As I noted in a prior article, I don’t love that the company is goodwill heavy because it could lead to impairment charges, yet I understand in Rollins' case goodwill will likely be larger given the amount of M&A activity occurring. Rollins is a solid cash-generating company, so overall I'm not too worried about the company's current liabilities or long-term debt, as the business has a good debt-to-equity balance with retained earnings continuing to grow.

Risks

Rollins has numerous risks, many of which are mentioned in the risk factors section of the company’s most current 10-K filing. I’m going to touch on a few that I believe are the most noteworthy.

First, Rollins is at risk of weather and climate changes and seasonality that impact the company’s bottom line. As noted this quarter, January was a particularly bad month for the company as they only saw organic growth of 6.6%, which is below the company’s estimates. Yet, this is the yearly lull for the business, so that’s to be expected. In March, growth greatly improved as the business had total growth of 12% and organic growth over 8%. I think most likely this trend will continue, and the business will see growth continue around this level, with January being an outlier.

As M&A is central to Rollins’ investment thesis, acquisition integration and company selection will be a risk for this business. Now, as Rollins is a very established and historic company, they have a history of making appropriate acquisitions, and they have a disciplined and strategic approach to their M&A activity. The business does have a sizable amount of goodwill on their balance sheet, as I noted, so a failure to successfully integrate an acquisition could lead to goodwill impairments hitting the P&L and negatively impacting earnings.

Valuation

Rollins has a valuation grade of an "F" at Seeking Alpha:

As Rollins is a profitable and established business, I believe price to earnings is the best valuation metric. On both a trailing and forward P/E GAAP basis, Rollins currently far exceeds the sector median. It’s not entirely shocking that Rollins has a much higher P/E compared to the sector median, as it’s a high-quality business, and thus many investors are willing to pay up.

On a historic basis, the current forward and trailing P/E GAAP for Rollins are lower than the company’s five-year averages, and if you look at the below, you see the company's forward P/E ratio (non-GAAP) has dipped below the 48-52 range that the business had been in for most of last year:

From another viewpoint, in using a reverse discounted cash flow model with a discounted rate of 10% and a terminal rate of 3%, Rollins would need assumed growth of over 18% to justify the current share price:

Analysts believe Rollins can grow in the high single digits over the next few years. I think that’s a reasonable assumption, yet I believe the business could get into double-digit growth with successful M&A acquisitions.

Conclusion

Rollins is a high-quality business that I believe long-term investors can buy and hold and sleep easy at night knowing this historic company isn’t going anywhere. With AI continuing to disrupt much of the tech industry, I don’t believe that’s something you can say about many businesses these days.

That being said, Rollins is expensive. However, it’s been expensive for a long time, as the company’s historic five-year averages illustrate. Thus, I believe investors should feel comfortable buying or at least establishing a position at these levels. While I've initiated a small position, I plan to add should the stock price fall back to or below $50.

data.title: Tesla Q1 Earnings: Catalysts Galore (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Tesla FQ1 Earnings Report

My last analysis on Tesla, Inc. (TSLA) stock was published on Mar 10 under the title of “Tesla: Poised To Start A New Wright's Law Curve.” The analysis focused on the updates of its Optimus Gen 3 robotics at that time, explored the potential of the company to start a new Wright’s Law-driven cost decline, and rated the stock as a buy. Since then, the company has issued its earnings report (ER) for FQ1 2026. Overall, I consider Tesla’s FQ1 2026 ER as a pivotal point marking its transition from a pure-play electric vehicle manufacturer to a diversified artificial intelligence company, a consideration that has prompted me to upgrade the rating to Strong Buy. Out of the many updates provided in the ER, this follow-up article will concentrate on 3 areas to stay on point: its margins, the growth of service revenues, and also the developments of its FSD (full self-driving).

As a brief recap of the ER, TSLA reported a normalized EPS of $0.41 on total revenue of $22.3 billion, as shown in the next chart. Both figures beat the consensus estimates of the Street. More importantly, both gross margins (21.2% on a GAAP basis) and operation margins (4.2%) showed a substantial YOY expansion of 478 and 214 bp (basis points), respectively, as seen. Next, I will detail the implications of these expansions and argue why I expect further profitability boosts ahead.

TSLA Stock: Growing Service Revenue Met Declining Carbon Credits

Out of the many factors that fueled the profit growth and margin expansion, the role of service revenues caught the most of my attention. As seen in the chart above, services and other revenues surged by 42% and hit $3.745 billion in the past quarter. These sales thus have grown to be sizable proportions, representing more than 23% of its automotive revenue (of $16.2 billion) and surpassing the sales from its energy generation and storage segment. This growth suggests to me that the company’s future is becoming increasingly decoupled from simple hardware volume.

The decoupling is further evidenced by—and/or is of particular importance because of—the role of the carbon credits in its profit streams. To wit, according to the following report, Tesla’s carbon credit totaled $1.993 billion in 2025, a large drop (of 28%) from its 2024 peak level of $2.763 billion. More observations from the data are quoted from the report below. I fully share the insight that Tesla’s further growth and/or margin will depend less and less on the volume growth of these credits (and the highlights in the quotes were added by me).

Thus, by prioritizing high-margin services over vehicle price cuts and/or regulatory credits (which are ultimately unpredictable), I see a brighter prospect of substantial EPS growth with enhanced margins and lower volatility (emphasis added):

The EV maker has confirmed its role in carbon credit pooling. This means it shares emissions credits with other automakers. This helps them meet regional rules, especially in Europe. Tesla sells extra zero-emission credits to partner automakers under pooling agreements. In return, they receive payments. The 2025 data shows that carbon credits are still high-margin and important. However, they no longer provide steady support each quarter. Their effect on operating margin now relies on timing, regulatory cycles, and year-end compliance needs, not steady growth.

TSLA FSD And Robotaxi Updates

As aforementioned, my overall impression from the ER is the transition of TSLA from an EV maker to a diversified artificial intelligence. My last article focused on the progress of its Optimus robotics. Here, I want to elaborate more on the progress of its Full Self-Driving (FSD) and also the monetization potential.

In Q1, TSLA continued seeing rapid growth of its FSD adoption. As shown in the next chart, since the introduction of its V12 and beyond platform, the total miles driven with FSD grew from about 1 billion miles in the March quarter of 2024 to more than 9 billion miles in the past quarter with an accelerating curve. In terms of subscriptions, TSLA reported record net new FSD additions of 180K in Q1, with total active subscriptions now reaching 1.28 million. To echo my above point, I expect such growth to trigger nonlinear EPS growth, as the marginal cost of FSD is near zero in my assessment. Unlike traditional manufacturing (of EV and/or energy storage units), where growth is linear and capital-intensive, the FSD software suite should operate on a near-zero marginal cost per additional user once the R&D threshold is cleared.

Other Risks And Final Thoughts

Another catalyst closely related to the FSD development is TSLA’s robotaxi. Per the Q1 ER, the company has deployed robotaxis in a total of 4 cities (SF Bay Area, Austin, Dallas, and Houston), with 3 of them operating in an unsupervised mode. The company is preparing to expand robotaxis to 5 additional markets currently. However, the expansion seems to be progressing slower than the timeline the company had announced earlier. In Q1, TSLA described these expansions as “preparations underway,” as seen in the ER slide below, a change from the 1H26 target announced earlier. I can see reasons why it’s a good idea to temper the pace of new rollouts until the safety of its v15 FSD software is thoroughly tested. But I did not hear any specific timeline provided for the v15 release in the ER.

In terms of valuation, the stock is pricey, especially in bottom-line-oriented ratios. As seen in the next chart, TSLA’s valuation ratios are quite expensive both in absolute and also relative terms. In absolute terms, its FY1 P/E ratio is now about 179x, a quite extreme reading. In relative terms, the other Mag 7 stocks are priced with P/E ratios “only” around 27x on average. However, for a high-growth company, I suggest investors look past the accounting P/E ratios and consider other metrics such as P/E based on owner’s earnings and growth-adjusted valuation ratios. These aspects were the focus of my other articles, and the valuation premium is far lower than what the headline P/E ratio suggests in my assessment. This link provides an example of my assessment of its owners’ earning P/E ratio. And as another reflection, its EV/sales and P/sales ratios are far better aligned with the other Mag 7 peers, as shown in the chart below.

To recap, this TSLA revisit was triggered by the updates in its Q1 ER. A few updates left an especially strong impression on me and signaled the transition of TSLA from a capital-intensive EV manufacturer to a high-margin physical AI powerhouse. I expect this transition to further deepen in the near future, to further expand its profit margin, and also to help shield its profit growth from unpredictable extrinsic factors. These considerations have led me to see a further skewness in its return/risk curve than at the time of my last writing and prompted me to upgrade my rating to Strong Buy.
